In conclusion, remote work can reduce commuting and widen location choices, but it cannot solve affordability by itself. Governments should treat it as one factor in regional planning while increasing housing supply, social provision and transport-linked development.

Why the exam-length essay is strong

Direct position

The introduction accepts that remote work can redistribute demand while keeping housing delivery central.

Causal explanation

The essay connects commuting flexibility, unequal access, regional demand and physical housing supply.

Developed contrast

Relocation options for remote workers are balanced against the needs of households tied to workplaces and services.

Policy mechanism

Zoning reform, social housing, office conversion and regional investment turn the position into a workable programme.

Recycled language

Earlier collocations return as part of the reasoning rather than as decoration.

Controlled complexity

Advanced grammar remains clear enough for realistic exam conditions.

7. Advanced grammar transformations

1. If cities approved more housing, fewer workers would be displaced. (Conditional inversion)

Were cities to approve more housing, fewer workers would be displaced.

2. The city changed zoning after rents had already risen sharply. (Past perfect)

The city changed zoning after rents had already risen sharply.

3. Stable housing matters most for family security. (Cleft sentence)

What matters most for family security is stable housing.

4. Residents will accept density only when infrastructure improves. (Negative inversion)

Only when infrastructure improves will residents accept density.

5. Remote work reduces commuting and changes housing demand. (Not only...but also)

Not only does remote work reduce commuting, but it also changes housing demand.

6. The office was designed for work, but it became expensive housing. (Participle clause)

Designed for work, the office eventually became expensive housing.

7. Although rent controls protect tenants, they may discourage supply. (Fronted concession)

Protective though rent controls may be, they may discourage supply.

8. Housing policy should expand supply, protect tenants and improve location. (Controlled parallelism)

Housing policy should expand supply, protect tenants and improve location.

9. Cities have added homes, but affordability remains weak. (Present-perfect contrast)

Cities have added homes, yet affordability remains weak.

10. The developer applied for permission after financing had become more expensive. (Past perfect)

The developer applied for permission after financing had become more expensive.

11. The planning system lacks capacity, so projects are delayed. (Nominalisation)

The planning system’s lack of capacity delays projects.

12. If public transport were stronger, more areas would become viable for housing. (Conditional inversion)

More areas would become viable for housing were public transport stronger.

13. Workers left the district because housing costs became unaffordable. (Cleft cause)

It was unaffordable housing that caused workers to leave the district.

14. Governments should increase supply and preserve housing quality. (Balanced recommendation)

Governments should both increase supply and preserve housing quality.

15. The city introduced parking reform gradually, so residents could adapt. (Participle clause)

Introducing parking reform gradually, the city gave residents time to adapt.

16. The council changed the plan after residents identified accessibility problems. (Emphatic do)

The council did change the plan after residents identified accessibility problems.

17. No factor matters more than well-located affordable housing. (Negative inversion)

Rarely does any factor matter more than well-located affordable housing.

18. The housing system should be affordable, flexible and inclusive. (Controlled parallelism)

The housing system should improve affordability, preserve flexibility and widen inclusion.

8. Native Academic Toolbox

1. Upgrade: “Housing is too expensive.” using housing affordability.

Housing affordability has deteriorated because prices and rents have outpaced incomes.

2. Upgrade: “There are not enough homes.” using housing shortage.

A persistent housing shortage is increasing competition across tenures.

3. Upgrade: “Planning rules stop apartments.” using exclusionary zoning.

Exclusionary zoning prevents diverse housing in high-opportunity neighbourhoods.

4. Upgrade: “People spend too much on rent.” using rent burden.

Severe rent burden forces households to reduce other essential spending.

5. Upgrade: “Young people cannot buy homes.” using mortgage affordability.

Weak mortgage affordability excludes many younger households from ownership.

6. Upgrade: “New homes take too long to approve.” using permitting delays.

Permitting delays raise financing costs and reduce the number of viable projects.

7. Upgrade: “Offices can become apartments.” using adaptive reuse.

Adaptive reuse can convert suitable commercial buildings into residential space.

8. Upgrade: “Remote workers move to cheaper towns.” using residential mobility.

Remote work increases residential mobility across metropolitan regions.

9. Upgrade: “Poor workers live far from jobs.” using spatial mismatch.

Spatial mismatch separates lower-income workers from employment and services.

10. Upgrade: “The city needs homes near stations.” using transit-oriented development.

Transit-oriented development can add homes while reducing car dependence.

11. Upgrade: “The government should build cheap homes.” using social housing.

Social housing provides stable rents for households excluded from market supply.

12. Upgrade: “Developers should pay for infrastructure.” using land-value capture.

Land-value capture can finance infrastructure created by planning gains.

13. Upgrade: “The neighbourhood needs several housing types.” using missing-middle housing.

Missing-middle housing expands options between detached houses and towers.

14. Upgrade: “Short-term rentals reduce normal rentals.” using rental supply.

Large-scale short-term rental activity can weaken long-term rental supply.

15. Upgrade: “Housing and transport policies should match.” using housing-transport policy alignment.

housing-transport policy alignment is essential across housing, transport and labour markets.

9. IELTS Speaking

Part 1 · 15 questions

PART 1 · 1

Do you prefer living in a city centre or outside it?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move inmove out
Model answer · 86 words

I prefer living close to the centre because shops, transport and cultural activities are easier to reach. However, high rent prices may force people to move out even when they value the location. I would move in only if the home was affordable, quiet and reasonably sized. A central address is not useful when housing costs consume most of a salary or the apartment provides no space for daily life. Access to parks and public transport would influence the decision as much as the postcode itself.

PART 1 · 2

Would you like to own a home?

Suggested phrasal verbs
lock outdrive down
Model answer · 83 words

I would like the security of home ownership, but I would not treat buying as the only respectable option. High deposits and borrowing costs can lock out people with stable incomes but little family wealth. More supply and fairer finance may drive down some barriers, although prices also depend on location and land. A well-regulated rental market can provide stability without forcing every household into a large mortgage. Ownership is valuable mainly when it provides stability rather than a speculative guarantee of wealth.

PART 1 · 3

Have rents changed in your area?

Suggested phrasal verbs
push upprice out
Model answer · 81 words

Rents have generally risen, especially for smaller homes in well-connected districts. Strong demand and limited rental supply push up prices, while newcomers compete with existing residents. This can price out students, young families and essential workers. The problem is not simply that landlords are greedy; construction, finance and regulation also affect supply. Still, tenants need protection from sudden increases and insecure contracts. Long-term contracts and predictable rules can reduce anxiety even when rents remain relatively high. That instability affects community life.

PART 1 · 4

Would remote work change where you live?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move outopen the way for
Model answer · 84 words

Full-time remote work could open the way for more locations because I would not need to commute every day. I might move out of an expensive centre to gain space, although reliable internet, transport and services would still matter. Hybrid work changes the calculation because occasional office trips can become exhausting from a distant town. The ideal choice would balance housing cost, social life and access rather than maximise floor space alone. Moving farther away can also weaken friendships and access to specialist services.

PART 1 · 5

Do you need a separate home office?

Suggested phrasal verbs
make availableplan for
Model answer · 82 words

A separate home office would be useful, but it is not essential for everyone. A foldable desk or shared room can make available more usable space in a small apartment. Developers could also plan for communal workrooms in residential projects, allowing residents to work nearby without paying for an extra bedroom. Housing design now needs to recognise that homes may support employment, study and family life at the same time. Shared facilities work only when booking, noise and maintenance are managed fairly.

PART 1 · 6

Would you live in a small apartment?

Suggested phrasal verbs
drive downmove in
Model answer · 85 words

I would live in a small apartment if the layout was efficient and the neighbourhood offered good public space. Smaller units can drive down monthly costs and allow more households to move in near jobs. However, extremely small homes become difficult when two adults work remotely or a family grows. Minimum standards for light, storage and ventilation are important because affordability should not mean accepting unhealthy or unusable housing. Flexible walls and storage can make compact homes far more functional than their floor area suggests.

PART 1 · 7

Is commuting time important when choosing housing?

Suggested phrasal verbs
open the way forpush up
Model answer · 83 words

For me, commuting time is one of the most important factors because a cheaper home may carry a large time cost. Good transport can open the way for more neighbourhoods to workers, but stations and new infrastructure may also push up nearby prices. I would compare the full journey, reliability and ticket cost rather than distance alone. Remote work reduces commuting, yet many services and social activities still depend on physical accessibility. Employers should also recognise that commuting costs differ sharply between workers.

PART 1 · 8

Would you live in public housing?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move inlock out
Model answer · 82 words

I would have no objection to public housing if it was well maintained and integrated with ordinary neighbourhoods. Governments need to build up enough supply that allocation is based on need rather than a desperate competition for very few units. Residents should be able to move in without stigma. Good public housing includes transport, schools and maintenance, not merely a cheap building placed far from employment. Mixed-tenure design can prevent public housing from becoming geographically isolated. Maintenance funding matters just as much.

PART 1 · 9

Do empty homes bother you?

Suggested phrasal verbs
make availablephase in
Model answer · 79 words

Empty homes are frustrating in places with a severe housing shortage, but vacancy has several causes. Some properties need repairs, are between tenants or are located where demand is weak. Cities can phase in higher taxes on long-term vacancy and use grants to make available habitable units. Enforcement should distinguish genuine temporary vacancy from homes deliberately held unused for speculation or occasional tourism. Public reporting would make it easier to see whether vacancy policies actually return homes to use.

PART 1 · 10

Would you support more apartments in your neighbourhood?

Suggested phrasal verbs
open the way fordrive down
Model answer · 82 words

I would support additional apartments if design, transport and local services were planned properly. Moderate density can build up the customer base for shops and public transport. Zoning reform can open the way for more homes on sites currently limited to one housing type. Residents should influence design, but existing owners should not receive a permanent veto over newcomers. A neighbourhood is a community, not a private club with inherited membership. New buildings should also contribute to schools, drainage and public space.

PART 1 · 11

Do you like mixed-use neighbourhoods?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move inhollow out
Model answer · 82 words

I prefer mixed-use development because homes, shops and workplaces create activity throughout the day. When offices dominate one district, remote work can hollow out local businesses after commuters disappear. New residents who move in can support cafés, services and safer streets in the evening. The mixture still needs careful management because nightlife, deliveries and housing may conflict, but separation is not always the solution. Ground-floor uses should reflect resident needs rather than serving only high-income visitors. That balance makes neighbourhoods more resilient.

PART 1 · 12

Would you consider living in a converted office?

Suggested phrasal verbs
convert intodrive down
Model answer · 81 words

I would consider it if the building had proper windows, ventilation and sound insulation. Some offices can convert into attractive apartments and reuse central locations. Conversion may drive down demolition waste, but it is not automatically cheap or suitable. Deep floor plates, structural changes and plumbing can make projects expensive. I would judge the final home, not the novelty of living in a former workplace. Safety standards must remain strict because adaptive reuse can involve complex structural work. Layout is decisive.

PART 1 · 13

Do short-term rentals affect your city?

Suggested phrasal verbs
push updo away with
Model answer · 82 words

In popular areas, short-term rentals can reduce the number of homes available to residents and push up rents. However, occasional home sharing is different from entire buildings operating like hotels. Cities should do away with unlicensed commercial activity while allowing limited use under clear rules. Enforcement needs platform data, because regulations are decorative when authorities cannot identify which properties are being rented repeatedly. Local residents still need legal certainty that enforcement will continue after political attention fades. Proportional rules would be reasonable.

PART 1 · 14

Would you move to a commuter town?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move outplan for
Model answer · 87 words

I might move out to one of the commuter towns if housing was substantially cheaper and transport remained reliable. The town would need schools, healthcare and social life rather than functioning only as a dormitory for the main city. Governments should plan for rail and local services before approving rapid growth. Otherwise, residents gain a larger home but spend years in congestion and place pressure on infrastructure that was designed for a smaller population. Remote workers might use local coworking spaces when occasional professional separation is needed.

PART 1 · 15

Has remote work improved city life?

Suggested phrasal verbs
make availablehollow out
Model answer · 88 words

Remote work has made more time available for many employees and reduced some peak commuting. It has also shifted spending towards residential neighbourhoods. On the other hand, it can hollow out office districts and create inequality between remote-capable professionals and on-site workers. The overall effect depends on whether cities adapt transport, housing and land use. Empty offices and crowded homes existing side by side would be a particularly human form of poor coordination. City policy should respond to changed patterns rather than trying to restore every pre-remote-work routine.

Part 3 · 15 questions

PART 3 · 1

What causes housing affordability crises?

Suggested phrasal verbs
push uplock out
Model answer · 120 words

Housing becomes unaffordable when demand grows faster than housing supply, incomes lag behind prices and finance becomes expensive. Land scarcity, slow planning, construction costs and low vacancy rates can push up rents and prices. Credit conditions may also lock out younger or lower-wealth households even when monthly payments appear manageable. The crisis is therefore not caused by one actor. It reflects labour markets, taxation, zoning, transport and investment. Effective policy must expand supply in suitable locations while protecting households facing immediate housing-cost overburden. Tax systems can worsen the imbalance when they reward property gains while penalising new construction or mobility. Labour-market concentration also matters because rapidly growing firms can create housing demand faster than construction systems and infrastructure can respond.

PART 3 · 2

Does building more housing always reduce prices?

Suggested phrasal verbs
plan fordrive down
Model answer · 126 words

Additional supply generally reduces pressure compared with a situation where the same demand meets fewer homes. Cities should plan for housing where jobs and services are growing. However, new luxury units may not immediately drive down rents for low-income households, especially in highly constrained markets. Construction takes time, and land costs can absorb much of the benefit. Governments therefore need both broad supply reform and targeted affordable housing. Filtering may improve affordability over years, while social housing and allowances protect people who cannot wait for a theoretical future equilibrium. Affordable requirements and public land can ensure that some of the new stock reaches households excluded from market prices. Inclusionary rules should be calibrated carefully, since obligations that make projects unviable produce no affordable units at all.

PART 3 · 3

Should cities relax zoning rules?

Suggested phrasal verbs
open the way forphase in
Model answer · 125 words

Cities should relax rules that unnecessarily prevent apartments, smaller lots or missing-middle housing in high-demand areas. Reform can open the way for more locations and reduce the power of existing owners to exclude newcomers. However, density should be coordinated with infrastructure, design and environmental constraints. Governments can phase in change near transport first and use land-value capture to finance services. Zoning reform is necessary but insufficient because permitting, finance and construction capacity also matter. The objective is not maximum density everywhere, but a wider range of homes in opportunity-rich areas. Resident consultation should improve form and infrastructure rather than become a procedural tool for indefinite delay. Reform should also address appeals, fragmented responsibilities and infrastructure connections, which often delay projects after zoning has already changed.

PART 3 · 4

Are rent controls useful?

Suggested phrasal verbs
price outphase in
Model answer · 126 words

Rent controls protect existing tenants when sudden increases threaten stability and price out households. They are especially defensible when contracts are insecure and supply cannot respond quickly. However, strict controls may discourage maintenance, reduce rental supply or create large differences between protected and new tenants. A balanced system can phase in limits on excessive increases while supporting construction and enforcing quality standards. Regulation should protect occupancy without freezing the entire market indefinitely. The design matters more than ideological labels, because weak tenant protection and badly designed controls can both create serious harm. Regular review is essential so emergency protections do not become permanent barriers to supply and mobility. Tenant databases and inspection capacity help prevent landlords from recovering controlled rent through hidden fees or neglected maintenance.

PART 3 · 5

Should governments build more social housing?

Suggested phrasal verbs
step up delivery ofconvert into
Model answer · 128 words

Governments should step up delivery of social housing because markets do not reliably provide secure homes for very low-income households. Public and non-profit providers can build up a permanent stock rather than paying rising subsidies into scarce private markets. Social housing also supports essential workers and people leaving homelessness. The risk is poor maintenance or concentration in isolated districts. Funding must therefore include management, renewal and mixed neighbourhoods. Public provision should complement private construction, not replace every tenure. Its value lies in creating a stable affordable option that remains outside speculative cycles. Housing associations and municipalities also need predictable finance because stop-start programmes raise costs and weaken delivery capacity. Allocation should remain transparent, with clear eligibility and routes for tenants whose income changes after they enter the system.

PART 3 · 6

Can office conversions solve housing shortages?

Suggested phrasal verbs
convert intomake available
Model answer · 122 words

Some vacant offices can be converted into useful housing, bringing underoccupied central buildings back into use. Adaptive reuse may reduce demolition and support struggling city centres. However, office layouts often have deep floors, limited windows, unsuitable plumbing or structural constraints. Conversion costs can exceed new construction, and not every location supports family housing. The policy should focus on buildings with realistic geometry and require normal residential standards. Conversions are a valuable part of supply, not a substitute for zoning reform, new construction or social housing. Structural development-risk appraisal is especially important when developers add floors or alter load-bearing systems. Cities should avoid granting large subsidies for projects that would proceed without support, because scarce public money has alternative uses. Design quality matters.

PART 3 · 7

How has remote work changed housing demand?

Suggested phrasal verbs
move outpush up
Model answer · 124 words

Remote work increases demand for extra rooms, quieter environments and reliable digital infrastructure. Some workers move out of expensive centres to suburbs or smaller cities, which can push up prices in previously cheaper areas. The effect is uneven because only certain occupations can work remotely. The resulting telework advantage may reinforce wealth and housing inequality. Hybrid schedules keep workers tied to metropolitan labour markets, so relocation often stretches urban regions. Housing and transport policy should therefore plan across functional urban areas, not only municipal borders. Remote work can also increase electricity and heating costs at home, transferring part of the workplace burden to employees. Employers may also change office attendance policies, making housing decisions based on remote work less secure than they initially appear.

PART 3 · 8

Can remote work reduce regional inequality?

Suggested phrasal verbs
open the way forhollow out
Model answer · 127 words

Remote work can open the way for people outside major centres to access high-paying jobs, while spending remains in smaller communities. However, it may also hollow out local labour markets if remote employees use metropolitan salaries to compete for limited housing while local wages remain lower. Digital access is necessary but not sufficient; schools, healthcare, transport and social networks influence relocation. Regional development should attract diverse employers and services rather than depend entirely on mobile professionals. Remote work can support rebalancing, but it cannot replace investment in local economic capacity. Smaller cities need safeguards against displacement so regional development benefits existing residents as well as newcomers. Local taxation can capture part of the increased demand and reinvest it in housing, transport and services for the wider community.

PART 3 · 9

Should cities convert parking spaces into housing?

Suggested phrasal verbs
do away withopen the way for
Model answer · 122 words

Where land is scarce and public transport is strong, cities should do away with excessive minimum parking requirements. This can open the way for housing on land previously reserved for parking and reduce construction costs. However, removing parking without transport alternatives may burden shift workers, disabled residents or families in poorly connected areas. Policy should reflect local conditions and price parking separately from housing where possible. The larger principle is that every parking requirement has a land-use trade-off. Urban land should not be reserved automatically for cars when people cannot find homes. Revenue from land or parking reform can finance accessible transport and neighbourhood improvements. Older requirements should be reviewed regularly because travel behaviour, vehicle ownership and street design change over time.

PART 3 · 10

How can housing support sustainable urbanisation?

Suggested phrasal verbs
plan fordrive down
Model answer · 119 words

Cities should plan for homes near public transport, employment and existing infrastructure rather than relying only on distant expansion. Strategies such as transit-oriented development, urban infill and mixed-use development can reduce travel demand and support services. Governments must also build up parks, schools and utilities alongside housing. Density alone is not sustainability if residents live in overheated buildings or lack public space. Compact growth works when homes are affordable, energy-efficient and connected. It should reduce both environmental pressure and spatial inequality, not merely increase land values around stations. Mixed-income housing can preserve access to high-opportunity districts and reduce concentrations of disadvantage. Retrofitting existing buildings is equally important because rapid urbanisation cannot rely entirely on new construction at the edge.

PART 3 · 11

Should short-term rentals be restricted?

Suggested phrasal verbs
do away withmake available
Model answer · 118 words

Commercial short-term rentals should be restricted in neighbourhoods with severe shortages because they can remove units from the rental market. Licensing and night limits may make available homes for residents. Cities can do away with illegal whole-property businesses while preserving occasional home sharing. The challenge is enforcement and proportionality. Tourism supports jobs, and some areas have sufficient capacity. Platform data, registration and local vacancy evidence should determine policy. Restrictions should target professional conversion of housing into accommodation rather than punish every resident renting a spare room. Rules should also distinguish primary residences from investment portfolios operating at hotel scale. Enforcement costs should be funded through platform fees or registration charges rather than left to already stretched local budgets.

PART 3 · 12

What makes housing policy socially fair?

Suggested phrasal verbs
lock outprice out
Model answer · 128 words

Fair policy recognises that housing markets distribute access to schools, transport, safety and employment. High deposits can lock out households without inherited wealth, while rising rents price out residents from opportunity-rich areas. Governments should combine supply, social housing, tenant protection and targeted assistance. They should also consider tenure-related distributional effects of taxes and planning decisions. A subsidy that inflates prices may benefit owners more than buyers. Fairness does not mean every person receives the same home; it means rules do not convert existing wealth into exclusion from urban opportunity. Accessibility, family size and disability should be considered, since affordability metrics based only on price overlook whether a home is usable. Intergenerational fairness matters as well, since policies protecting existing owners can shift every adjustment cost onto younger households.

PART 3 · 13

Can housing allowances solve affordability?

Suggested phrasal verbs
drive downpush up
Model answer · 119 words

Housing allowances protect incomes and can reduce immediate rent burden. They are flexible because households use existing homes rather than waiting for construction. However, when supply is extremely limited, subsidies may push up rents and transfer public money to landlords. They do not directly drive down the underlying shortage. Allowances should therefore be targeted and combined with new supply, quality enforcement and rent data. They remain essential for vulnerable households, but demand-side support cannot substitute indefinitely for increasing the stock of affordable homes. Administrative simplicity matters because households facing insecurity may struggle with complex applications and repeated documentation. Benefit levels should reflect local prices, but national funding may be needed where high-cost cities would otherwise bear the entire burden.

PART 3 · 14

How should cities deal with vacant offices?

Suggested phrasal verbs
convert intohollow out
Model answer · 119 words

Cities should assess buildings individually. Suitable offices can convert into apartments, hotels, schools or mixed uses, helping prevent districts from being hollowed out. Others may remain valuable for future employment or require demolition. Policy can offer tax relief, flexible zoning and faster approvals while preserving structural and fire safety. Ground floors and public space also matter because residential conversion alone does not create a complete neighbourhood. The objective should be adaptive urban recovery rather than forcing every empty office through the same fashionable solution. Public agencies should publish conversion costs and outcomes so incentives are adjusted through evidence rather than optimism. Conversion programmes should also track who can afford the resulting homes, because reuse alone does not guarantee affordability.

PART 3 · 15

What would a successful housing system look like?

Suggested phrasal verbs
step up delivery ofdrive down
Model answer · 128 words

A successful system would step up delivery of housing in places where people need it while offering stable choices across ownership, private rent, social housing and cooperative models. It would gradually drive down housing-cost overburden without sacrificing quality. Planning would connect homes with transport and public services, while assistance protected households during shocks. Success would also mean lower homelessness, shorter waiting lists and greater residential mobility. Housing would still be an asset, but it would function first as a home, not the main mechanism through which wealth and opportunity are inherited. Relevant long-run housing outcomes should include security, health and access to opportunity, not only total units completed. A successful system would allow people to form households, change jobs and age without housing insecurity controlling every major decision.

10. Five IELTS Writing Task 2 topics

Before writing: check that each body paragraph has a clear topic sentence, explanation, development and a relevant consequence or example. Your position must remain consistent from the introduction to the conclusion.
TASK 2 · 1

Cities should remove most restrictions on apartment construction in areas with severe housing shortages. To what extent do you agree or disagree?

Optional collocation bank
housing shortageexclusionary zoninglock outmissing-middle housingopen the way forplanning legitimacyland-value captureinclusionary zoninghousing affordability
Model essay · 318 words

Cities with serious housing shortage should reform rules that prevent apartments, although I do not support removing every restriction. The objective should be faster, more diverse housing in suitable locations rather than construction without planning.

Existing rules often protect low density near transport and employment. Exclusionary zoning, large parking requirements and lengthy approval can lock out new residents. What restrictive planning preserves is not merely neighbourhood character but scarcity. Allowing missing-middle housing, apartments and accessory units can increase choice without requiring towers on every street. More construction can also support shops and public transport. If cities open the way for housing near stations, households gain access to jobs with lower travel costs. However, growth requires schools, utilities and public space. Removing rules without infrastructure planning can create congestion and weaken planning legitimacy.

Some restrictions remain necessary. Building safety, flood risk, daylight and accessibility should not be abandoned. Historic or ecologically sensitive areas may justify stronger limits. Were all regulation treated as obstruction, cities could produce unsafe homes and irreversible environmental damage. Reform should therefore be targeted. Municipalities can set clear design codes, approve compliant projects quickly and use land-value capture for infrastructure. Inclusionary zoning or public land can ensure that part of the new supply remains affordable.

Existing residents deserve consultation, but not an unlimited veto over newcomers. Appeals and repeated hearings should focus on genuine planning issues. Affordability should also be monitored after construction. New zoning may increase land prices before homes are completed, benefiting existing owners. Public agencies can capture part of this gain and invest it in affordable units or local infrastructure. Reform should include anti-displacement measures where redevelopment threatens low-income tenants. Greater supply is essential, but communities should not experience improvement only as removal.

In conclusion: cities should remove many restrictions that block apartments in high-demand areas. They should retain rules protecting safety and public value while replacing discretionary delay with predictable, evidence-based planning.

TASK 2 · 2

Some people believe rent controls are necessary to protect tenants, while others think they reduce housing supply. Discuss both views and give your opinion.

Optional collocation bank
rent pricesprice outrental marketresidential mobilityphase inpush uphousing affordabilityhousing shortagehousing supply
Model essay · 320 words

Rent controls are among the most disputed housing policies. Supporters view them as essential protection against displacement, while critics argue that they discourage investment. In my view, moderate regulation is necessary, but it must be combined with new supply and maintenance standards.

The strongest argument for controls is tenant stability. When rent prices rise suddenly, households may be priced out of communities near work and schools. Limits on excessive annual increases allow families to plan and reduce involuntary movement. What rent regulation protects is not only income but continuity of daily life. However, strict controls can create problems. Owners may withdraw units from the rental market, reduce maintenance or favour short-term contracts. A large difference between controlled and market rents can also reduce residential mobility, because tenants are reluctant to leave a protected unit even when it no longer suits them.

The solution is balanced design. Governments can phase in predictable increase limits, require just cause for eviction and enforce quality. Regulation should permit reasonable returns and renovation costs while preventing hidden fees. Not only must tenants receive security, but landlords must also have incentives to maintain and supply homes. Controls should be reviewed against vacancy, construction and affordability data. In severe shortages, social housing and zoning reform remain necessary because regulation does not create units.

Housing allowances can support low-income tenants, although poorly targeted subsidies may push up rents where supply is fixed. Data systems are important because governments need reliable information on rents, evictions and building condition. Registration can improve enforcement without requiring every tenant to begin a legal dispute individually. Emergency controls should also include clear review dates. Permanent rules should be based on evidence about supply and tenant welfare rather than political habit.

In conclusion: rent controls are useful as protection against extreme instability but harmful when treated as a complete housing strategy. Moderate regulation, enforcement and expanded supply can protect tenants without freezing the market.

TASK 2 · 3

Converting empty offices into homes is becoming more common. Do the advantages outweigh the disadvantages?

Optional collocation bank
office conversionconvert intohollow outdevelopment-risk appraisalhousing affordabilityhousing shortagehousing supplyhousing stockrental supply
Model essay · 306 words

Empty offices and housing shortages have encouraged cities to promote office conversion. This approach offers important environmental and urban benefits, but its success depends on building design and public standards.

The first advantage is reuse. Existing structures can convert into apartments without complete demolition, reducing waste and preserving central locations. New residents may revive businesses in office districts that have been hollowed out by remote work. What conversion can achieve is a simultaneous response to underused offices and unmet housing demand. However, offices are not designed as homes. Deep floors may lack windows, while plumbing, ventilation and fire escape systems require extensive work. Recent engineering problems have also shown the importance of structural development-risk appraisal. Only when a building is physically suitable should public incentives support conversion.

Costs are another concern. Complex alterations can make converted apartments expensive. Tax relief may therefore subsidise luxury housing rather than affordability. Cities should require transparent feasibility studies and link public support to clear outcomes. Conversions work best as part of a mixed strategy. Ground floors can provide shops or community services, while neighbouring public space is improved. Some offices should remain workplaces, and others may be better used for education or demolition and reconstruction.

Environmental benefits should also be measured rather than assumed, because major structural work can consume substantial materials. The social composition of the resulting housing matters. Central conversions can create access to transport and services, but only if rents are not restricted to high-income households. Public finance should therefore purchase affordability or community facilities, not merely private feasibility. Cities should publish completed costs and performance so future incentives reflect actual experience.

In conclusion: office-to-housing conversion can add homes and revive city centres. Its advantages outweigh the disadvantages for suitable buildings, provided that safety, affordability and neighbourhood planning are treated as normal requirements rather than inconvenient details.

TASK 2 · 4

Essential workers are increasingly unable to afford housing near the places where they work. What problems does this cause, and what solutions should governments introduce?

Optional collocation bank
spatial mismatchaffordable housinginclusionary zoningsocial housingopen the way forprice outhousing affordabilityhousing shortagehousing supply
Model essay · 310 words

Teachers, nurses, care workers and transport staff are increasingly unable to live near their workplaces. This creates problems for public services, household welfare and urban inequality.

Long commutes increase fatigue, transport costs and staff turnover. Schools and hospitals may struggle to recruit, while workers lose time with family. What expensive housing creates is a labour shortage even when qualified people exist. The problem also reinforces spatial mismatch, because low-paid employees travel from distant areas to serve high-cost neighbourhoods. Governments should increase affordable housing near employment centres. Public land can support mixed-tenure projects, and inclusionary zoning can reserve units within private development. Social housing eligibility should recognise essential occupations without excluding households facing even deeper poverty.

Transport remains important but cannot replace housing. Faster rail can open the way for more areas, yet long journeys still impose time costs. Employers may provide temporary accommodation, but this should not become a substitute for functioning housing markets. Tenant protection and targeted allowances can prevent workers being priced out during sudden rent increases. Not only should governments support workers’ incomes, but they should also expand the homes available near jobs.

Regional planning is necessary because municipalities may approve employment growth while neighbouring areas carry the housing burden. Infrastructure funding should follow new development. Employers also have responsibilities. Large institutions can contribute land, long-term leases or investment to workforce housing, provided allocation remains fair and employees are not tied to a job through insecure accommodation. Housing near work should include family-sized and accessible units rather than only small studios designed around one ideal worker. Public agencies should publish vacancy and commuting data so housing investment follows actual workforce needs rather than political anecdotes.

In conclusion: unaffordable housing weakens essential services and deepens inequality. Well-located affordable housing, tenant stability and coordinated transport can reduce the problem more effectively than expecting workers to accept indefinitely longer commutes.

TASK 2 · 5

How has remote work changed city centres and suburban areas? What policies should governments adopt in response?

Optional collocation bank
hollow outmove outplan forconvert intohousing affordabilityhousing shortagehousing supplyhousing stockrental supply
Model essay · 323 words

Remote work has changed the geography of urban activity. City centres receive fewer daily commuters, while residential suburbs and smaller towns experience greater daytime demand. Governments should adapt rather than attempting to restore every previous pattern.

Central business districts may be hollowed out as office workers stay home. Restaurants and transport systems lose revenue, while empty offices weaken street activity. At the same time, remote employees spend more in neighbourhood shops and may move out to larger homes in suburban areas. What has shifted is the location of demand, not the need for urban services. Suburban growth can increase housing prices, car use and pressure on schools. Hybrid work still requires occasional commuting, so distant expansion may create irregular but intense travel demand. Governments should plan for housing and public transport together rather than approve isolated estates.

City centres need more mixed use. Suitable offices can convert into housing, education or cultural space. Streets designed mainly for commuters can gain public space and local services. Office attendance has fallen, yet many central districts have remained governed by land-use rules written for the old pattern. Policy should also protect on-site workers. Cutting transit because professional commuters travel less may harm people who still depend on daily service. Remote-work benefits should not be financed through worse access for others.

Regional tax and planning systems can share the costs of population change. Smaller communities receiving new demand need infrastructure and affordable housing. Housing design must respond to work at home. New projects may include flexible rooms or shared workspaces, while older housing needs reliable broadband and energy efficiency. These improvements should not become premium features available only to high earners. Planning should operate across metropolitan boundaries because remote-work movement ignores municipal borders.

In conclusion: remote work has weakened some city-centre activity while strengthening residential areas. Governments should encourage mixed-use centres, coordinated suburban growth and equitable transport rather than forcing workers or buildings back into obsolete routines.

Course index · Module 1 · Chapter 13
